Subsequent global phosphotyrosine proteomics (using LC-MS/MS) and functional signaling assays (e.g., MAPK, PI3K/Akt activity, receptor ubiquitination/degradation) in mutant cells will identify compensatory pathways activated upon Y1112 loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of compensatory phosphotyrosine interactions within the ErbB-2 network, defining its inherent robustness. It will elucidate molecular mechanisms that may underpin residual signaling in targeted therapies, offering new conceptual frameworks for understanding resistance and identifying potential combination targets.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely applies a network ecology analogy of keystone species loss and compensatory interactions to a precise molecular signaling problem, moving beyond the study of single sites to interrogate system-level redundancy and plasticity in ErbB-2 signaling.

proposals.title: Functional Redundancy in ErbB-2 Downregulation: Systematic Analysis of Tyrosine Residues Compensating for the Loss of c-Cbl Binding at Y1112
proposals.motivation: ErbB-2 (HER2) overexpression drives aggressive breast cancers, and its downregulation via c-Cbl-mediated ubiquitination is a critical regulatory checkpoint. Pre-2026 work established Y1112 as a primary c-Cbl binding site, but the receptor's robust signaling network suggests potential compensatory mechanisms. Understanding if other phosphotyrosines can recruit c-Cbl upon Y1112 mutation is essential to explain resistance to therapies targeting ErbB-2 degradation and to inform the development of more durable treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the absence of Y1112, one or more alternative tyrosine residues on ErbB-2 can become phosphorylated and serve as secondary docking sites for the c-Cbl E3 ubiquitin ligase, facilitating receptor downregulation and maintaining signaling homeostasis.
proposals.method: We will employ site-directed mutagenesis to create a panel of ErbB-2 constructs: a Y1112F single mutant, a 'phosphorylation-dead' mutant with all cytoplasmic tyrosines mutated to phenylalanine, and a series of 'add-back' mutants where individual tyrosines are restored. These will be expressed in TNBC cell models. Using co-immunoprecipitation and proximity ligation assays, we will quantify c-Cbl binding and ubiquitination. Functional readouts will include receptor degradation kinetics (cycloheximide chase), downstream MAPK/PI3K pathway activation (phospho-specific immunoblotting), and cell proliferation/survival assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the ErbB-2 phosphotyrosine landscape for c-Cbl interaction, revealing potential redundant regulatory nodes. It will provide a mechanistic framework for understanding innate or acquired resistance to therapies that rely on ErbB-2 degradation. The findings could identify new combinatorial therapeutic targets to block compensatory downregulation pathways in HER2+ and TNBC cancers.
proposals.novelty: While Y1112's role is known, a systematic, residue-by-residue investigation of functional redundancy within ErbB-2's cytoplasmic tail for c-Cbl binding has not been performed. This approach moves beyond the canonical site to explore the network plasticity of receptor regulation.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of E3 Ubiquitin Ligases Mediating Antibody-Induced Degradation of ErbB-2 in Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Therapeutic antibodies like trastuzumab induce ErbB-2 degradation, a key anti-tumor mechanism, but the underlying ubiquitination machinery is incompletely defined. Pre-2026 work primarily implicates the E3 ligase c-Cbl, yet redundancy and compensatory pathways in cancer could limit therapeutic efficacy and drive resistance, especially in TNBC where ErbB-2 signaling is complex. A comprehensive understanding of all recruited E3 ligases is crucial for developing more effective degradation-based therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: While c-Cbl is a primary mediator, other E3 ubiquitin ligases are recruited by anti-ErbB-2 antibodies to facilitate receptor ubiquitination and degradation, and their contribution becomes critical upon c-Cbl inhibition or loss.
proposals.method: We will employ a targeted siRNA screen against a curated library of ~600 human E3 ligases in ErbB-2+ breast cancer cells (e.g., SKBR3, BT474) treated with trastuzumab or the antibody-drug conjugate T-DM1. Degradation will be monitored by immunoblotting and flow cytometry. Hits will be validated via CRISPR-Cas9 knockout and reconstitution with ligase-dead mutants. Proximity ligation assays (PLA) and co-immunoprecipitation will confirm antibody-induced recruitment of candidate E3s to ErbB-2. Kinase activity and downstream signaling (PI3K/Akt, MAPK) will be assessed to link ligase function to therapeutic effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the E3 ligase landscape for antibody-induced ErbB-2 degradation, identifying novel therapeutic targets to enhance receptor downregulation. It will provide mechanistic insight into potential resistance pathways and inform the design of combination therapies or next-generation biologics that optimally engage the ubiquitin-proteasome system.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond the established role of c-Cbl to systematically test the long-standing assumption of its exclusivity, using modern functional genomics in a therapeutically relevant context to uncover redundant or compensatory E3 ligase networks.

proposals.title: Systematic Surveillance of c-Cbl/ErbB-2 Pathway Mutations to Define the Landscape of Resistance to HER2-Targeted Antibody Therapies
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of HER2-targeted antibodies like trastuzumab in breast cancer, intrinsic and acquired resistance remains a major clinical problem. Pre-2026 work from the Yarden lab and others has established that the E3 ubiquitin ligase c-Cbl is critical for ligand-induced degradation of ErbB-2, and defects in this pathway can drive therapeutic resistance. However, a comprehensive, systematic catalog of clinically relevant mutations in this degradation axis, analogous to surveillance of antibiotic resistance in bacteria, is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tumors resistant to HER2 antibody therapies harbor a defined spectrum of mutations and expression alterations in the ErbB-2 c-Cbl binding interface and the c-Cbl gene itself, and that these molecular lesions correlate with the degree of resistance and poor patient outcomes.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-exome and RNA sequencing on a well-annotated cohort of pre-treatment and post-progression tumor biopsies from patients with HER2+ breast cancer, including TNBC subsets. Using established bioinformatics pipelines (e.g., GATK for variant calling, DESeq2 for expression analysis), we will systematically map all non-synonymous mutations, copy number alterations, and expression changes in ERBB2 (focusing on the c-Cbl binding tyrosine 1112 region) and CBL. Mutation prevalence and co-occurrence will be correlated with in vitro resistance data from patient-derived organoids and clinical progression-free survival.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first high-resolution map of 'resistance hotspots' within the ErbB-2 degradation machinery, moving beyond candidate gene studies. It will establish a direct clinical link between specific c-Cbl/ErbB-2 pathway defects and therapeutic failure, providing a rationale for stratifying patients and for developing next-generation therapies, such as PROTACs or specific kinase inhibitors, that bypass these defects.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a population genetics-based surveillance paradigm, standard in microbiology, to a solid tumor oncogene, and in focusing systematically on the degradation pathway—a key resistance mechanism—rather than just canonical kinase domain mutations.

proposals.title: Defective c-Cbl-Mediated ErbB Receptor Downregulation Underlies Adult Tissue Repair Deficits in Germline Kit/Kit Ligand Mutant Mice
proposals.motivation: Germline mutations in the receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) Kit or its ligand (Steel) are well-studied for developmental defects, but their impact on adult tissue homeostasis and repair is poorly understood. Given the central role of RTK signaling dynamics, particularly ligand-induced degradation via E3 ubiquitin ligases like c-Cbl, in controlling cellular responses, a defect in this pathway could explain post-developmental pathologies. This is highly relevant to cancer biology, as c-Cbl-mediated downregulation is a known mechanism of action for therapeutic anti-ErbB antibodies, suggesting potential pharmacological interventions for repair deficits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mutant Kit signaling environment in adult stem/progenitor cells compromises the recruitment of c-Cbl to ligand-activated ErbB family receptors (specifically EGFR or ErbB-3), impairing their ubiquitination and lysosomal degradation. This defective receptor clearance leads to sustained, aberrant ErbB signaling that disrupts the regenerative capacity of epithelial or hematopoietic tissues in adult W/Wv or Sl/Sld mice.
proposals.method: We will use primary keratinocytes and bone marrow-derived progenitor cells from W/Wv, Sl/Sld, and wild-type mice. Receptor dynamics will be analyzed by stimulating cells with EGF or heregulin, followed by immunoprecipitation and western blotting to assess c-Cbl recruitment, receptor ubiquitination, and degradation kinetics. Signaling output will be measured via phospho-kinase arrays and targeted assays for key pathways (e.g., MAPK, AKT). Functional repair capacity will be tested in vivo using a cutaneous wound healing model and a 5-FU-induced myelosuppression model, with or without pharmacological inhibition of ErbB signaling (e.g., lapatinib) or proteasomal degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, non-developmental consequence of germline Kit pathway mutations, linking them to defective RTK turnover and adult tissue repair. It will mechanistically connect the Kit signaling environment to the regulation of other RTK families, specifically the ErbBs. The findings could reveal a pharmacologically targetable vulnerability (i.e., hyperactive ErbB signaling) to improve tissue regeneration in individuals with such mutations.
proposals.novelty: While c-Cbl's role in RTK downregulation is known, its functional impairment as a *trans*-regulatory consequence of a different germline RTK mutation in adult tissue repair is unexplored. This moves beyond the developmental paradigm of W/Sl mutations and directly tests a mechanistic bridge to ErbB signaling, a core expertise of the Yarden lab.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Visualization of c-Cbl Recruitment to ErbB-2 by Therapeutic Antibodies Using smFRET-TIRF Microscopy
proposals.motivation: The ErbB-2 receptor tyrosine kinase is a major oncogenic driver in breast cancer, particularly TNBC. Therapeutic antibodies like trastuzumab induce ErbB-2 downregulation, a process critically dependent on the E3 ubiquitin ligase c-Cbl, but the precise spatiotemporal dynamics of this key interaction remain a black box. Understanding the initiation of this downregulation pathway at the single-receptor level is essential for deciphering mechanisms of resistance and improving therapeutic efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that therapeutic antibody binding to ErbB-2 triggers rapid, quantifiable recruitment of c-Cbl to individual receptors at the plasma membrane, and that the kinetics and stoichiometry of this interaction, visualized at the single-molecule level, correlate with the efficiency of subsequent receptor internalization and degradation.
proposals.method: We will adapt single-molecule co-localization FRET (smFRET) methods, as pioneered for studying host-virus protein interactions. ErbB-2 and c-Cbl will be tagged with the FRET pair Cy3 (donor) and Cy5 (acceptor) in live breast cancer cells (e.g., BT-474, SK-BR-3). Using total internal reflection fluorescence (TIRF) microscopy, we will image individual receptors at the basal membrane. Custom particle tracking and FRET efficiency calculation algorithms (based on established IDL or MATLAB routines for single-molecule analysis) will quantify the timing, duration, and spatial distribution of c-Cbl-ErbB-2 binding events before and after trastuzumab treatment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, real-time visualization of the critical c-Cbl-ErbB-2 interaction, establishing a quantitative framework for the initial steps of antibody-induced downregulation. It will identify kinetic parameters (e.g., binding rates, complex stability) that define an effective downregulation signal, offering new molecular metrics to evaluate and engineer next-generation ErbB-2 targeted therapies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the methodological adaptation of a sophisticated virology tool (smFRET for protein recruitment) to solve a long-standing problem in receptor tyrosine kinase trafficking, enabling observation of a key therapeutic interaction at an unprecedented single-molecule, millisecond resolution in live cancer cells.

proposals.title: Deciphering the Degradative Code: High-Resolution Mapping of Trastuzumab-Induced Ubiquitin Chain Topology on ErbB-2 in TNBC Models
proposals.motivation: The therapeutic antibody trastuzumab, targeting the receptor tyrosine kinase ErbB-2, is a cornerstone of breast cancer treatment, yet resistance, particularly in aggressive subtypes like triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), remains a major clinical challenge. Pre-2026 work indicates that trastuzumab's efficacy is linked to ErbB-2 internalization and degradation via ubiquitination, but the precise molecular 'code'—the specific lysine residues modified and the linkage types (e.g., K48, K63, K11) of the attached ubiquitin chains—remains a black box. Defining this code is critical, as different chain topologies dictate distinct cellular fates, such as proteasomal degradation versus altered signaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that trastuzumab binding induces a specific, non-random pattern of ErbB-2 ubiquitination, characterized by defined lysine acceptor sites and a predominance of K48-linked polyubiquitin chains, which is essential for its efficient downregulation and anti-tumor activity in TNBC models.
proposals.method: We will employ state-of-the-art quantitative mass spectrometry (MS) coupled with immunoaffinity purification. ErbB-2 will be immunoprecipitated from trastuzumab-treated versus control TNBC cell lines (e.g., MDA-MB-453). Following tryptic digestion, ubiquitinated peptides will be enriched using anti-diGly remnant antibodies (K-ε-GG). MS analysis will be performed on a high-resolution instrument (e.g., Orbitrap Fusion Lumos) using Higher-Energy Collisional Dissociation (HCD) and Electron-Transfer/Higher-Energy Collision Dissociation (EThcD) to preserve labile ubiquitin linkages. Data will be processed using MaxQuant software with integrated ubiquitin remnant motif search to map modification sites, and specialized algorithms like Ubiquitin Chain Enrichment by Middle-Down Mass Spectrometry (UbiChEM-MS) or spectral matching to synthetic ubiquitin chain standards will be used to assign linkage types.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, site- and linkage-specific map of ErbB-2 ubiquitination triggered by a therapeutic antibody. It will mechanistically link trastuzumab's action to a defined degradative ubiquitin signature, offering novel biomarkers for predicting response. Furthermore, it will establish a robust MS pipeline for analyzing drug-induced ubiquitination of other receptor tyrosine kinases, advancing the field of targeted protein degradation therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While ubiquitination of ErbB-2 is known, the precise topology induced by trastuzumab has never been elucidated at the level of individual acceptor lysines and chain linkages. Applying cutting-edge middle-down/top-down MS proteomics to solve this specific problem in the context of a clinically critical antibody-receptor pair represents a significant technical and conceptual advance beyond existing bulk biochemical studies.

proposals.title: A Fragment-Based Docking Strategy to Model the Ternary Trastuzumab-ErbB-2-c-Cbl Interface and Predict Therapeutic Efficacy
proposals.motivation: The therapeutic antibody trastuzumab induces ErbB-2 internalization and degradation via recruitment of the E3 ubiquitin ligase c-Cbl, a key mechanism in breast cancer treatment. However, the structural basis of this critical ternary complex remains poorly resolved, limiting our ability to model resistance mechanisms or design improved therapies. Computational modeling is challenged by the size and flexibility of the full antibody-antigen interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, fragment-based docking approach, analogous to strategies in fragment-based drug discovery, can accurately model the ternary trastuzumab-ErbB-2-c-Cbl complex by first solving a constrained intracellular anchor point.
proposals.method: We will first perform high-rigidity protein-peptide docking using HADDOCK or Rosetta FlexPepDock to model the interaction between the c-Cbl SH2 domain and a phosphorylated ErbB-2 cytoplasmic tail peptide (pY1139). This solved intracellular 'fragment' complex will then serve as a spatial constraint for subsequent protein-protein docking of the trastuzumab Fab fragment against the ErbB-2 extracellular domain using ZDOCK and refinement with ClusPro. Molecular dynamics simulations (AMBER) will assess interface stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computationally derived structural model of the complete therapeutic degradation complex, offering mechanistic insights into trastuzumab's action. It will establish a novel computational pipeline for modeling large, multi-protein therapeutic interfaces, applicable to other receptor tyrosine kinase systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the conceptual framework of fragment-based drug discovery—using a small, well-defined interaction as an anchor—to the distinct problem of modeling a large antibody-induced intracellular signaling complex, a strategy not previously applied in this context.

proposals.title: Lineage-Correlated Single-Molecule Fate Mapping of ErbB-2 Reveals Ubiquitination Dynamics as a Determinant of Cell Fate in Mammary Morphogenesis
proposals.motivation: Receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) signaling dynamics, particularly ligand-induced ubiquitination and degradation, are critical for cell fate decisions in development and cancer. However, it remains unknown whether stochastic variation in the processing of individual RTK molecules within sister cells is a cause or a consequence of divergent cell fates. Resolving this is essential to understand how robust tissue patterns emerge from noisy molecular events and has direct implications for the dysregulated RTK trafficking seen in cancers like TNBC.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cell-to-cell variability in the ubiquitination and endocytic fate of individual, ligand-activated ErbB-2 molecules within a dividing lineage is a primary driver, rather than a passive reflection, of divergent signaling outputs and subsequent cell fate decisions during mammary epithelial morphogenesis.
proposals.method: We will establish a dual-modality live-cell imaging system in primary murine mammary organoids. Using CRISPR-Cas9-mediated endogenous tagging, we will label ErbB-2 with a photoconvertible fluorescent protein (e.g., mMaple) for single-molecule tracking and a ubiquitination-specific fluorescent biosensor (based on tandem ubiquitin-binding entities). Concurrently, we will perform single-cell lineage tracing using a non-overlapping fluorescent histone label and a Cre-loxP-based heritable barcoding system. Automated image analysis pipelines (leveraging TrackMate and custom algorithms for lineage reconstruction) will correlate the lifetime, diffusion coefficient, and ubiquitination status of individual ErbB-2 molecules with real-time ERK and AKT biosensor activity and the ultimate differentiation state (luminal vs. basal) of each tracked cell within the lineage tree.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first causal map linking the stochastic behavior of individual RTK molecules to cell fate specification in a developing tissue. It will define precise kinetic parameters of RTK ubiquitination that bias lineage choices, offering a new quantitative framework for developmental biology. The findings will provide a foundational mechanistic understanding of how RTK trafficking fidelity is maintained, with direct translational relevance to therapies targeting RTK degradation in breast cancer.
proposals.novelty: While single-molecule tracking and lineage tracing exist separately, their fusion to interrogate the causal role of molecular stochasticity in fate decisions is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond correlative snapshots to establish a temporal and causal chain from single-molecule event to population-level patterning.

proposals.title: Synthetic Chaperone Antibodies to Stabilize ErbB-2 by Blocking c-Cbl-Mediated Degradation in TNBC Models
proposals.motivation: In TNBC and other cancers, ErbB-2 (HER2) overexpression drives tumorigenesis, but its turnover is regulated by c-Cbl-mediated ubiquitination and lysosomal degradation. Current therapeutic antibodies like trastuzumab target the extracellular domain and can induce receptor downregulation, but there is no tool to specifically block degradation while preserving kinase activity to study the direct consequences of stabilized, signaling-competent receptor pools on tumor biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering intracellular antibody fragments (scFvs) that bind with high affinity to the cytoplasmic c-Cbl docking site on ErbB-2 will act as synthetic chaperones, sterically blocking ubiquitin ligase access, thereby stabilizing the receptor and amplifying its oncogenic signaling output in a controlled manner.
proposals.method: We will employ phage display libraries to select scFvs against a peptide encompassing the known c-Cbl binding motif (Tyr1112) in the ErbB-2 cytoplasmic tail. High-affinity binders will be validated for specificity via ELISA and co-immunoprecipitation in HEK293T cells. Selected scFvs will be expressed intracellularly in TNBC cell lines (e.g., MDA-MB-231) using lentiviral vectors. Their impact on ErbB-2 stability (half-life via cycloheximide chase), ubiquitination status, downstream kinase activity (MAPK/PI3K pathways), and phenotypic outcomes (proliferation, invasion) will be quantified.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first targeted molecular tool to decouple ErbB-2 degradation from its signaling, allowing precise dissection of the role of receptor stabilization in oncogenesis. It will establish a novel 'stabilization therapy' proof-of-concept, contrasting with degradation-inducing therapies, and provide insights into resistance mechanisms in ErbB-2-driven cancers.
proposals.novelty: While intracellular antibodies (intrabodies) have been used to inhibit kinases, the application as a synthetic chaperone to specifically shield a degradation motif, thereby *enhancing* oncogenic signaling for mechanistic study, is a novel and inverse approach to ErbB-2 modulation.

proposals.title: A Modular Degradation Platform: Engineering c-Cbl-Recruiting Adaptors for Targeted Oncoprotein Clearance in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation offers a promising therapeutic strategy for cancers driven by overexpressed receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs), such as EGFR and c-MET in TNBC. However, current approaches like PROTACs require de novo development for each target and face pharmacological challenges. A universal, modular degradation system inspired by the adaptable architecture of CAR-T cells could provide a flexible platform to rapidly target multiple oncogenic drivers, addressing tumor heterogeneity and adaptive resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthetic bifunctional adaptors, which physically link a target-specific binding domain to a high-affinity c-Cbl recruitment module, can function as a universal platform to induce the degradation of diverse oncogenic RTKs by hijacking endogenous E3 ligase machinery.
proposals.method: We will engineer a library of 'Degradation Recruiting Adaptors' (DeGRAs) comprising: 1) variable targeting moieties (e.g., anti-EGFR nanobodies, c-MET-binding peptides), 2) a flexible linker, and 3) a conserved, optimized domain derived from Cbl-b that binds and maximally activates c-Cbl's E3 ligase function. These constructs will be expressed in TNBC cell lines. Degradation efficiency will be assessed via immunoblotting and cycloheximide chase assays. Functional impact will be measured by phospho-kinase arrays, proliferation, and 3D spheroid invasion. In vivo efficacy will be tested using orthotopic TNBC PDX models expressing DeGRAs via viral vectors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for a novel, modular protein degradation platform technology. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of c-Cbl-mediated degradation of multiple RTKs and deliver pre-clinical validation for a flexible strategy to combat oncogenic signaling in TNBC and other RTK-driven cancers.
proposals.novelty: While PROTACs and related molecules exist, the conceptual translation of the CAR-T's modular, 'one core, many targets' logic to create a universal E3 recruitment platform for intracellular oncoproteins is novel. Specifically, the engineered, high-affinity exploitation of endogenous c-Cbl as a constant degradation effector represents a distinct approach from using cereblon or VHL.

proposals.title: Targeted Degradation of Oncogenic Receptor Tyrosine Kinases via Engineered c-Cbl Recruiters: A Strategy for TNBC and Beyond
proposals.motivation: Receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) like EGFR, MET, and AXL are frequently overexpressed or mutated in cancers, including triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), driving tumor progression and therapy resistance. Current targeted therapies, such as kinase inhibitors and monoclonal antibodies, often face limitations due to acquired resistance and incomplete target inhibition. Harnessing endogenous cellular degradation machinery, specifically the E3 ubiquitin ligase c-Cbl, offers a novel therapeutic paradigm to eliminate oncogenic RTKs more completely.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that developing bifunctional small molecules or peptides that directly recruit c-Cbl to specific, overexpressed oncogenic RTKs (beyond ErbB-2) will induce their ubiquitination and lysosomal degradation, leading to potent anti-tumor effects in preclinical models.
proposals.method: We will employ structure-based design and high-throughput screening to identify compounds or peptides that bind the tyrosine kinase-binding (TKB) domain of c-Cbl. These will be conjugated to ligands for target RTKs (e.g., EGFR, MET) to create Proteolysis-Targeting Chimeras (PROTACs) or peptide-based degraders. Candidate molecules will be validated for target engagement, ubiquitination, and degradation in TNBC cell lines using immunoblotting and cycloheximide chase assays. Anti-tumor efficacy and impact on the tumor microenvironment will be assessed in patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models of TNBC, with single-cell RNA sequencing to evaluate changes in immune cell infiltration.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for targeted protein degradation by directly engaging c-Cbl, a natural regulator of RTK turnover. It will provide proof-of-concept for degrading a broader spectrum of oncogenic RTKs, potentially overcoming limitations of current inhibitors. The findings could lead to new therapeutic strategies for TNBC and other RTK-driven cancers.
proposals.novelty: While PROTACs typically recruit cereblon or VHL E3 ligases, directly recruiting the endogenous RTK-specific ligase c-Cbl is a novel approach that may offer greater specificity and leverage natural regulatory pathways. This strategy moves beyond inhibiting kinase activity to actively eliminating the oncogenic protein.

proposals.title: Comparative in vivo analysis of TCRζ versus FcRγ signaling domains in CAR-T cells for the treatment of TNBC: Impact on efficacy, toxicity, and TME remodeling
proposals.motivation: Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy faces challenges in solid tumors like triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), including poor persistence and severe toxicities like cytokine release syndrome. The choice of intracellular signaling domain is critical, with CD3ζ (TCRζ) being standard and FcRγ being explored for potentially distinct signaling outputs. A direct, systematic comparison of these domains in immunocompetent, relevant in vivo models is lacking but essential for optimizing next-generation CAR designs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CAR-T cells incorporating the FcRγ signaling domain will exhibit a differentiated functional profile in vivo compared to those using TCRζ, leading to altered therapeutic efficacy, a distinct toxicity profile, and differential remodeling of the tumor microenvironment (TME) in TNBC models.
proposals.method: We will engineer second-generation CARs targeting a validated TNBC antigen (e.g., ROR1) with identical scFv and co-stimulatory (CD28) domains but differing only in the primary signaling domain (TCRζ vs. FcRγ). Using syngeneic (4T1) and patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models of TNBC in immunocompromised and humanized mice, we will compare tumor growth kinetics, CAR-T persistence (via bioluminescence), and systemic toxicity (cytokine levels, clinical scores). We will analyze TME infiltration and immune composition by flow cytometry and spatial transcriptomics, focusing on myeloid subsets and redox states, given the AhR's known role in BRCA1-associated TMEs.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, side-by-side in vivo evaluation of TCRζ vs. FcRγ signaling in a therapeutically relevant TNBC context. It will establish a functional link between CAR signaling architecture and specific TME modulation, informing the rational design of safer, more effective CAR-T cells for solid tumors. The work will directly extend Dr. Yarden's expertise in receptor tyrosine kinase signaling and the tumor microenvironment to the field of cellular immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While the domains have been studied in isolation, a direct in vivo comparison in solid tumor models, coupled with deep analysis of TME remodeling—particularly linking CAR signaling to AhR-pathway-associated redox and myeloid states—is a novel and mechanistically insightful approach.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Functional Screen to Define Affinity-Specificity Trade-offs in anti-HER2 scFv Clones for Targeted Therapy in TNBC
proposals.motivation: Despite HER2 being a validated target in breast cancer, its low-level expression in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (TNBC) and other solid tumors presents a therapeutic challenge, as high-affinity antibodies can cause on-target, off-tumor toxicity. Pre-2026, the precise relationship between single-chain variable fragment (scFv) affinity, specificity for tumor-overexpressed HER2 versus normal tissue levels, and resultant therapeutic efficacy in aggressive cancers like TNBC remains poorly quantified. This gap hinders the rational design of next-generation antibody-based therapeutics with optimized safety profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an intermediate-affinity anti-HER2 scFv exists within a diverse phage display library that optimally balances high tumor uptake in HER2-low TNBC models with minimal retention in normal tissues expressing physiological HER2 levels, thereby maximizing the therapeutic window.
proposals.method: We will employ a yeast surface display library of diverse anti-HER2 scFv clones for high-throughput screening. Clones will be sorted based on binding to recombinant HER2 ectodomain across a gradient of antigen densities using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) to mimic tumor (high density) versus normal (low density) expression. Selected clones will be characterized for kinetic rate constants (kon, koff, KD) via surface plasmon resonance (SPR). The top candidates will be reformatted into IgG-like bispecific T-cell engagers (BiTEs) targeting HER2 and CD3ε and tested for cytotoxicity in co-cultures of primary human T-cells with HER2-low TNBC cell lines (e.g., MDA-MB-231) versus non-transformed mammary epithelial cells (MCF-10A).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative framework linking scFv biophysical parameters to functional outcomes in a therapeutically relevant context. It will identify lead scFv candidates for HER2-targeting in TNBC and provide generalizable principles for engineering antibodies targeting antigens with a narrow expression window between tumor and normal tissue.
proposals.novelty: While anti-HER2 scFv affinity maturation is well-studied, this proposal uniquely focuses on de-optimizing affinity for safety in HER2-low contexts and employs a functional screen integrating antigen density discrimination with T-cell redirected killing, a critical metric for modern bispecific therapies, which is non-trivial and not extensively explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Engineering a Bifunctional ErbB-Targeting Chimeric Receptor for Autonomous Cytokine Amplification in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) remains a therapeutic challenge due to its aggressive nature and lack of targeted therapies. While receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) like EGFR/ErbB1 are frequently overexpressed in TNBC, their inhibition often leads to resistance. Simultaneously, the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME) limits the efficacy of immunotherapies. There is a critical need for novel strategies that directly target tumor cells while actively reprogramming the local TME to enhance anti-tumor immunity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering a chimeric receptor that couples ErbB-targeting with ligand-induced, localized secretion of a potent immunostimulatory cytokine (e.g., IL-2) will simultaneously inhibit oncogenic signaling and convert the tumor niche into a pro-inflammatory microenvironment, leading to superior anti-tumor activity.
proposals.method: We will construct a dual-function chimeric receptor by fusing an ErbB-specific single-chain variable fragment (scFv) to the transmembrane and intracellular domains of an activating immune receptor (e.g., CD3ζ). A synthetic promoter responsive to the receptor's activation signals (e.g., NFAT or NF-κB response elements) will be placed upstream of the IL-2 gene and integrated into the same lentiviral vector. This construct will be transduced into primary human T cells or NK cells. Functionality will be validated in vitro using TNBC cell lines, assessing ErbB-dependent receptor activation, downstream kinase signaling (phospho-ERK, phospho-AKT), controlled IL-2 secretion, and autocrine/paracrine T-cell proliferation. Efficacy will be tested in vivo using patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models of TNBC.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a novel class of 'smart' cellular therapeutics that autonomously sense tumor-associated RTKs and respond with localized immunomodulation. It will provide mechanistic insight into coupling targeted inhibition with microenvironmental reprogramming. The approach could offer a new strategy to overcome resistance to both RTK inhibitors and conventional immunotherapies in solid tumors.
proposals.novelty: Unlike conventional CAR-T cells or cytokine delivery systems, this design integrates the tumor-targeting and signaling-inhibition function of an ErbB-directed receptor with a feedback loop for in-situ cytokine production, creating a self-amplifying, localized anti-tumor response specifically triggered by the tumor cell itself.

proposals.title: Structural and Functional Definition of the p85 Regulatory Subunit Binding Motif within the Kit Kinase Insert Domain
proposals.motivation: The receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) Kit is a critical oncogenic driver in gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs) and other malignancies. Its signaling is tightly regulated by the recruitment of downstream effectors like the p85 subunit of PI3K, which binds to phosphorylated tyrosine residues within Kit's kinase insert (KI) domain. Precisely defining this interaction motif is essential for understanding oncogenic Kit signaling and for designing targeted inhibitors that could disrupt this specific pathway without affecting other RTK-p85 interactions, offering a more selective therapeutic strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the p85 binding motif on Kit comprises a specific, phosphorylated tyrosine residue within the KI domain, flanked by a unique amino acid sequence that confers high-affinity and selective binding to the p85 SH2 domains, distinct from motifs found in other RTKs like EGFR/ErbB family members.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of site-directed mutagenesis of the human Kit KI domain and peptide array screening to systematically map the critical residues. Binding affinity will be quantified using surface plasmon resonance (SPR) with purified p85 SH2 domains and synthetic phosphopeptides. The structural basis of the interaction will be elucidated by solving the co-crystal structure of the p85 N-terminal SH2 domain bound to the high-affinity Kit phosphopeptide using X-ray crystallography. Functional validation will be performed in TNBC cell models expressing wild-type or motif-mutant Kit to assess downstream PI3K/Akt pathway activation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution structural definition of the Kit-p85 interface, a key node in oncogenic Kit signaling. It will establish a precise binding consensus sequence, enabling the rational design of competitive peptide or small-molecule inhibitors. Furthermore, it will advance the fundamental understanding of how divergent RTKs, like Kit versus EGFR, achieve signaling specificity through unique adaptor recruitment motifs.
proposals.novelty: While p85 binding to phosphorylated RTKs is a general paradigm, the exact structural determinant on Kit remains undefined. This proposal moves beyond mere identification of phosphorylation sites to a mechanistic dissection of the specific sequence and structural motif, offering a template for isoform-specific pathway disruption compared to broad PI3K inhibitors.

proposals.title: In vivo dissection of EGFR-ERBB2 heterodimer signaling through kinase domain mutations: Impact on mammary tumorigenesis and the tumor microenvironment
proposals.motivation: The ErbB family of receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs), particularly EGFR and ERBB2, are key drivers in breast cancer, including TNBC. While their dimerization and kinase activation are well-studied in vitro, the physiological consequences of specific signaling perturbations within the native tissue context of a living organism remain poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to develop therapies that precisely target oncogenic RTK signaling networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted mutations in the kinase insert domain (KID) of EGFR, which is critical for substrate recognition and docking, will selectively disrupt specific downstream signaling pathways in vivo, thereby altering mammary tumor initiation, progression, and the associated tumor microenvironment (TME) in a BRCA1-deficient background.
proposals.method: We will generate transgenic mouse models with BRCA1 loss combined with either wild-type EGFR or EGFR containing point mutations in the KID (e.g., Y696F, Y920F) known to disrupt binding of specific substrates like PLCγ or Shc. Tumorigenesis will be monitored in these models. Tumors and adjacent stroma will be analyzed using phospho-proteomics to map pathway-specific signaling, multiplex immunohistochemistry to characterize immune infiltrates (e.g., T cells, macrophages), and RNA-seq to assess changes in the TME, linking specific KID-mediated signals to AhR pathway activation and redox homeostasis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct causal link between a defined structural module of an RTK (the EGFR KID), specific downstream signaling outputs, and the resulting physiological tumor phenotype and TME composition. It will reveal whether targeting specific docking interactions, rather than global kinase activity, offers a viable therapeutic strategy with potentially fewer side effects.
proposals.novelty: While KID functions are mapped biochemically, their role in shaping whole-organism physiology, particularly the TME in a relevant genetic cancer model, is unexplored. This approach moves beyond cell-autonomous signaling to investigate how a single RTK structural domain orchestrates the complex tumor ecosystem.

proposals.title: Combinatorial Antibody Screening Against Receptor Tyrosine Kinase Networks to Overcome Adaptive Resistance in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of monoclonal antibodies targeting Receptor Tyrosine Kinases (RTKs) like EGFR and HER2, resistance remains a major clinical challenge, particularly in aggressive cancers like Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (TNBC). Pre-2026 work demonstrates that tumors adapt via compensatory signaling through other RTKs and the tumor microenvironment, limiting the efficacy of single-agent therapies. A systematic approach to identify synergistic antibody combinations that simultaneously stimulate anti-tumor immunity and inhibit oncogenic signaling is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rationally designed combinations of stimulatory antibodies (e.g., targeting immune checkpoints or agonistic receptors) and inhibitory antibodies (e.g., blocking EGFR, HER3, or c-Met) will produce a supra-additive inhibitory effect on TNBC growth by disrupting compensatory signaling networks and reshaping the tumor microenvironment.
proposals.method: We will establish a high-throughput screening platform using a library of validated inhibitory (e.g., cetuximab, trastuzumab) and stimulatory (e.g., anti-CD40, anti-OX40, anti-PD-1) antibodies. Combinations will be tested in vitro on a panel of patient-derived TNBC organoids and co-cultures with immune cells, measuring cell viability, phospho-RTK signaling arrays, and cytokine secretion. In vivo validation will be performed in immunocompetent BRCA1-deficient and TNBC mouse models, using tumor growth kinetics and multiplex immunohistochemistry for immune infiltration analysis as primary endpoints. A factorial design and Bliss independence model will be used to score synergistic interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a prioritized list of clinically actionable antibody combinations for TNBC, moving beyond single-target inhibition. It will provide mechanistic insight into how co-targeting RTK networks and immune modulators alters the tumor ecosystem. The findings could directly inform the design of novel combination therapies for clinical trials.
proposals.novelty: While combination therapies are common, a systematic screen pairing stimulatory and inhibitory antibodies specifically within the RTK signaling context is novel. This approach integrates Yarden's expertise in RTK biology with emerging immuno-oncology concepts to tackle adaptive resistance holistically.

proposals.title: Development of Function-Selective Anti-ERBB2 Antibodies to Block Oncogenic Heterodimerization in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: ERBB2 (HER2) drives tumorigenesis primarily through heterodimerization with other ERBB family members, particularly EGFR and ERBB3, leading to potent kinase activation and resistance to current therapies. In triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), ERBB2 heterodimers are a key signaling node despite low overall expression, contributing to aggressive disease. Existing ERBB2-targeted antibodies like trastuzumab primarily block homodimerization and induce internalization but are less effective at specifically inhibiting heterodimer-driven signaling, creating a critical therapeutic gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by screening for antibodies that bind to distinct epitopes on the extracellular domain of ERBB2, we can identify clones that specifically and potently disrupt its heterodimerization with EGFR and ERBB3, thereby suppressing downstream oncogenic signaling more effectively than broad dimerization inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will employ a structure-guided phage display library screen using recombinant ERBB2 extracellular domain (ECD) as bait, with negative selection against ERBB2 homodimers to bias for heterodimer-disrupting clones. Positive hits will be validated using a suite of pre-2026 established assays: (1) Co-immunoprecipitation and FRET-based dimerization assays in TNBC cell lines (e.g., MDA-MB-231) to quantify inhibition of ERBB2-EGFR/ERBB3 complexes; (2) Phospho-kinase array and Western blotting to assess specific downregulation of AKT and MAPK pathways versus trastuzumab; (3) In vivo efficacy testing in patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models of TNBC with detectable ERBB2 heterodimers.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel class of function-selective anti-ERBB2 antibodies with a defined mechanism of action. It will provide a precise therapeutic tool to dissect the contribution of specific ERBB2 heterodimers to TNBC pathogenesis and potentially offer a new targeted strategy for a subset of TNBC patients currently lacking effective options.
proposals.novelty: While antibody therapies against ERBB2 exist, the deliberate screening for epitopes that selectively disrupt heterodimerization over homodimerization is a novel, mechanism-based approach. This moves beyond broad receptor blockade towards precision interference with specific protein-protein interactions that drive oncogenic signaling.

ideas.title: Identify ligand for Neu/ErbB-2 receptor
ideas.description: Discover the natural ligand that binds and activates the Neu/ErbB-2 receptor tyrosine kinase.

ideas.title: Cell-autonomous Kit signaling blockade as a sufficient and targeted therapeutic paradigm
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether a therapeutic monoclonal antibody that inhibits Kit receptor tyrosine kinase signaling without inducing its degradation is sufficient for anti-tumor efficacy, specifically in cancers driven by cell-autonomous Kit signaling. We hypothesize that in developmental or oncogenic contexts where Kit's function is strictly cell-autonomous—affecting only the lineage expressing it—mere signal blockade within the tumor cell will be as effective as degradation-based mechanisms. This approach challenges the prevailing focus on antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity or receptor degradation, proposing a more targeted strategy that could reduce systemic toxicity. The project will first map Kit signaling autonomy in relevant cancer models and then directly compare the anti-tumor effects of a purely inhibitory antibody versus a degradation-competent one.

ideas.title: Calibrate Oncogenic Signaling by Coupling Engineered Receptor Stabilization with Systematic Mutation Severity Mapping
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the systematic mapping of mutation severity with the engineering of antibodies that prevent ErbB-2 degradation. The core strategy is to use the engineered stabilizing antibodies to create a controlled, signaling-competent receptor overexpression baseline. Against this stabilized background, a panel of W/Sl mutations with defined molecular lesions (e.g., impaired kinase activity, altered docking sites) will be introduced. This allows for the precise titration of signaling output—from hyper-stabilized wild-type levels down through graded loss-of-function—directly correlating the severity of the molecular defect with phenotypic outcomes like transformation potency and pathway activation in a context where degradation is uncoupled as a variable. The synergy enables a quantitative 'dose-response' analysis of oncogenic signaling, revealing how tumors might evolve under selective pressure for optimized, rather than maximal, pathway activity.

ideas.title: Investigate heterodimerization with other ErbB members

ideas.sourceTitle: Agonistic antibodies stimulate the kinase encoded by the neu protooncogene in living cells but the oncogenic mutant is constitutively active.

ideas.title: Investigate c-Cbl-mediated receptor downregulation as a mechanism for adult tissue repair deficits in germline W/Sl mutation backgrounds
ideas.description: This project investigates whether germline mutations in Kit (W) or Kit Ligand (Sl), known for developmental roles, impair adult tissue repair by disrupting c-Cbl-mediated downregulation of ErbB family receptors. We hypothesize that the mutant RTK signaling environment in adult stem/progenitor cells compromises the recruitment of c-Cbl and subsequent ubiquitination of EGFR or ErbB-3/4, which is a mechanism leveraged by therapeutic antibodies. The study will test if defective receptor clearance via this pathway leads to aberrant signaling and failed regeneration in adult epithelial or hematopoietic tissues, revealing a pharmacologically targetable post-developmental consequence of these mutations.

ideas.title: Adapting Bacterial Antibiotic Resistance Surveillance to Map c-Cbl/ErbB-2 Mutation Landscapes in Therapy-Resistant Tumors
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from bacterial antibiotic resistance surveillance to the problem of clinical resistance to antibody therapy in tumors. In infectious disease, systematic genomic sequencing of resistant clinical isolates is used to map the prevalence and impact of resistance-conferring mutations. Adapted to this research context, the project would involve performing comprehensive genomic and transcriptomic analysis on tumor biopsies from patients with antibody-resistant HER2+ cancers to systematically catalog all mutations and expression changes in the ErbB-2 c-Cbl binding site and the c-Cbl gene itself. By correlating these mutational landscapes with quantitative measures of therapeutic resistance and patient outcomes, the research aims to identify high-frequency 'resistance hotspots' and mechanistic patterns, ultimately guiding the development of next-generation therapies that can overcome these specific degradation pathway defects.

ideas.title: Isolating pure signaling blockade: Testing antibody efficacy with genetically ablated receptor internalization.
ideas.description: This study directly addresses the critical confounding factor of receptor recycling by genetically disabling the tumor cell's capacity for receptor internalization. Using CRISPR-Cas9, key endocytic machinery components are knocked out in relevant cancer cell lines, creating models where receptors are trapped on the cell surface. The anti-tumor efficacy of a pure signaling-blockade antibody (engineered to avoid internalization and degradation) is then rigorously tested in these engineered models versus wild-type cells in vitro and in vivo. This approach cleanly separates the contribution of simple occupancy and signaling inhibition from the effects of receptor removal, providing a definitive test of the original hypothesis.

ideas.title: Optimize scFv affinity and specificity for therapy
ideas.description: Systematically test different anti-Neu/HER2 scFv clones for optimal tumor targeting and minimal off-target effects.
ideas.sourceTitle: Targeting of T lymphocytes to Neu/HER2-expressing cells using chimeric single chain Fv receptors.

ideas.title: Inverse: Engineer antibodies that prevent degradation by mimicking a molecular chaperone strategy.
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept by drawing an analogy from protein engineering and chaperone mimicry. The analogous problem—stabilizing a specific protein conformation by shielding a vulnerable region—is solved by designing synthetic chaperones that bind protective epitopes. Adapted to this context, the research would involve engineering ErbB-2-specific antibody fragments (e.g., scFvs) that function as synthetic chaperones. These fragments would be selected or designed to bind with high affinity to the precise cytoplasmic epitope encompassing the c-Cbl binding site, physically blocking the E3 ligase's access while leaving the kinase domain and other signaling interfaces fully operational. This would create a system to study the effects of stabilized, signaling-competent receptor overexpression by directly preventing the initiation of the degradation cascade, rather than interfering with downstream ubiquitination or lysosomal processes.

ideas.title: Single-Molecule Fate Mapping of Receptor Tyrosine Kinases in Single-Cell Lineages
ideas.description: This research direction combines single-molecule tracking of receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) like Kit or ErbB-2 with single-cell lineage tracing in embryonic development. It aims to track the ubiquitination and degradation fate of individual, ligand-activated RTK molecules within living cells of a known lineage, correlating these stochastic molecular events with real-time signaling dynamics and ultimate cell fate choices. By observing the same molecules in sister cells as they diverge, the approach will reveal if differential receptor processing is a cause or consequence of fate specification. This creates a causal map from single-molecule behavior to population-level tissue patterning.

ideas.title: Define precise role of kinase insert domain
ideas.description: Determine how the kinase insert domain specifically contributes to kinase activation and downstream signaling in Kit heterodimers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Dimerization and activation of the kit receptor by monovalent and bivalent binding of the stem cell factor.

ideas.title: Tyrosine 1112 is the sole critical site.
ideas.description: Investigate if other ErbB-2 tyrosine residues can compensate or mediate c-Cbl binding if Y1112 is mutated.
ideas.sourceTitle: Tumor-inhibitory antibodies to HER-2/ErbB-2 may act by recruiting c-Cbl and enhancing ubiquitination of HER-2.

ideas.description: Add a cytokine gene (e.g., IL-2) under the control of the chimeric receptor's activation signal.

ideas.title: c-Cbl is the only relevant E3 ligase.
ideas.description: Test if other E3 ubiquitin ligases can be recruited by the antibody to ubiquitinate and degrade ErbB-2.
ideas.sourceTitle: Tumor-inhibitory antibodies to HER-2/ErbB-2 may act by recruiting c-Cbl and enhancing ubiquitination of HER-2.

ideas.title: Lineage-Specific Receptor Degradation Thresholds Governed by E3 Ligase Specificity
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether the apparent equivalence of Kit signaling thresholds across erythrocyte, melanocyte, germ cell, and mast cell lineages is actually governed by differential recruitment of specific E3 ubiquitin ligases, like c-Cbl. We hypothesize that each lineage may utilize a unique, dominant E3 ligase (or set of ligases) to ubiquitinate Kit, creating lineage-specific degradation thresholds that shape the duration and amplitude of the signal. This would be tested by engineering Kit variants or using targeted recruitment tools to force the engagement of non-canonical E3 ligases in each lineage and measuring the consequent impact on differentiation and survival. The synergy lies in moving from asking 'how much signal' to asking 'which termination mechanism,' linking cell fate diversity to the specificity of protein degradation machinery.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Molecule Co-localization FRET from Virology to Visualize c-Cbl-ErbB-2 Interaction Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a methodological solution from virology—specifically, the use of single-molecule fluorescence co-localization and FRET (smFRET) to visualize the real-time recruitment of host cell factors to viral proteins during assembly—to the problem of visualizing c-Cbl recruitment to ErbB-2. In virology, this approach successfully mapped the stepwise interaction of proteins like TSG101 with HIV-1 Gag. The adapted method involves tagging ErbB-2 and c-Cbl with a donor-acceptor FRET pair (e.g., Cy3 and Cy5) and employing total internal reflection fluorescence (TIRF) microscopy on live cells. This setup will enable the direct visualization and quantitative analysis of the timing, spatial distribution, and dynamics of c-Cbl binding to individual ErbB-2 receptors at the plasma membrane following therapeutic antibody binding, offering unprecedented resolution into the initiation of receptor downregulation.

ideas.title: Adapting Molecular Fragment Docking from Drug Discovery for Antibody-c-Cbl Interface Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Fragment-Based Drug Discovery (FBDD) to the problem of modeling the ternary antibody-ErbB-2-c-Cbl complex. In FBDD, the computational docking of small molecular fragments to identify binding hot spots is a established method to overcome the challenges of docking large, flexible compounds. The adapted research idea applies this hierarchical docking strategy: first, perform protein-peptide docking simulations to model the core interaction between a phosphorylated ErbB-2 cytoplasmic tail motif and the c-Cbl SH2 domain. This provides a structurally constrained 'anchor' for the intracellular complex. Second, use this anchor pose to inform and guide subsequent protein-protein docking simulations of the full-length antibody against the extracellular domain of ErbB-2, thereby modeling the complete ternary interface in a more computationally efficient and accurate stepwise manner.

ideas.title: Functional Redundancy and Network Rewiring in a Phosphotyrosine Signaling Network
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that Y1112 is the sole critical site for c-Cbl binding on ErbB-2 by drawing an analogy from ecology, where the loss of a keystone species prompts investigation into compensatory network interactions. The analogous solution involves systematic node deletion and interaction mapping to uncover redundancy and network rewiring. Adapted to this molecular context, the research would involve creating a comprehensive panel of ErbB-2 tyrosine-to-phenylalanine mutants, then using quantitative binding assays and phosphoproteomic profiling to construct a complete binding network. This would determine if other tyrosine residues can functionally compensate for Y1112 mutation, either through direct low-affinity binding or by activating alternative signaling pathways that indirectly recruit c-Cbl, thereby revealing the robustness and plasticity of the ErbB-2 signaling network.

ideas.title: Apply receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor screening
ideas.description: Use high-throughput kinase inhibitor libraries to identify epiregulin-specific signaling dependencies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Epiregulin is Up-regulated in pancreatic cancer and stimulates pancreatic cancer cell growth.

ideas.title: Characterize the p85 binding motif on Kit
ideas.description: Define the precise amino acid sequence motif in the Kit kinase insert recognized by the p85 subunit.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interkinase domain of kit contains the binding site for phosphatidylinositol 3' kinase.

ideas.title: Direct Identification and Kinetic Characterization of SH2 Domain Proteins as Primary Substrates of Kit Kinase Activity
ideas.description: This redesigned study aims to definitively identify which cytoplasmic SH2 domain-containing proteins are direct phosphorylation targets of the activated Kit receptor tyrosine kinase, bypassing confounding cellular signaling networks. Using a purified, active Kit kinase domain and a recombinant library of human SH2 domain proteins in a controlled in vitro system, we will perform direct kinase assays to map primary substrate relationships. Phosphorylation events will be quantified via radiometric or phospho-specific antibody arrays, followed by precise phospho-site mapping via mass spectrometry. The approach will yield a validated, hierarchical list of direct Kit substrates with associated kinetic parameters, providing a foundational map for understanding immediate Kit signaling initiation.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune Recruitment: Using c-Cbl as a Universal Degradation Tag for Oncoproteins
ideas.description: This idea adapts the modular targeting principle from synthetic immunology, specifically Chimeric Antigen Receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy, to the problem of degrading diverse oncoproteins. In CAR-T, a customizable extracellular binding domain redirects a constant cytotoxic intracellular machinery to different cancer antigens. Analogously, we propose developing a platform of synthetic, bifunctional 'Degradation Recruiting Adaptors' (DeGRAs). Each DeGRA molecule would consist of a variable targeting moiety (e.g., a peptide, small molecule, or nanobody) specific to a chosen overexpressed oncogenic receptor (e.g., EGFR, c-MET, FGFR), fused via a optimized linker to a conserved, high-affinity c-Cbl recruitment domain. This domain would be engineered to maximally engage c-Cbl's RING finger E3 ligase activity. By administering or expressing these adaptors, any oncoprotein bound by the targeting moiety would be forcibly brought into proximity with c-Cbl, leading to its ubiquitination and degradation, thus scaling the original dual-targeting concept into a platform technology for multiple cancer drivers.

ideas.title: Inducible cell-ablation mosaic system to dissect autonomy in W/Sl mutant environments
ideas.description: This approach integrates mosaic analysis with inducible, specific ablation of mutant cells to definitively separate cell-autonomous from non-autonomous effects. A mouse model carrying W or Sl mutations flanked by Dre recombinase sites is crossed to a Cre-dependent diphtheria toxin receptor (DTR) line, with all alleles under tamoxifen control. Initial mosaic analysis characterizes the tissue. A second tamoxifen pulse then activates DTR exclusively in mutant cells, triggering their ablation. The subsequent phenotype of the remaining wild-type cells, now in a normalized environment, reveals whether their original defects were cell-autonomous or induced by the mutant niche.

ideas.title: Quantitative cell-autonomous phenotyping of W/Sl allelic series in genetic mosaics
ideas.description: This fused approach combines genetic mosaic analysis with systematic molecular phenotyping to create a high-resolution map of cell-autonomous function across the W/Sl allelic series. By generating chimeric mice where mutant cells (carrying precisely characterized molecular lesions in c-Kit or its ligand) compete with wild-type cells in a single animal, we can quantify the cell-autonomous contribution of each mutation to melanocyte, hematopoietic stem cell, and germ cell development. This allows us to directly link the biophysical severity of a signaling defect—such as reduced kinase activity or ligand binding affinity—to its functional consequence in vivo, controlling for non-cell-autonomous and systemic factors. The synergy reveals whether specific molecular lesions have differential impacts on distinct cell lineages and identifies critical signaling thresholds required for normal development and tissue maintenance.

ideas.title: Adapting a Plant Pathogen Effector-Target Mapping Strategy to Identify Kit-Phosphorylated SH2 Proteins
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from plant pathology, where researchers identify host proteins targeted by bacterial effector proteins by comparing interactions with functional versus non-functional effector mutants. The analogous problem here is identifying the specific cytoplasmic SH2 proteins that are phosphorylated upon Kit receptor activation, as opposed to those that merely bind to it. The adapted solution involves expressing a kinase-inactive (dead) mutant Kit receptor alongside the wild-type in a cellular model. Following receptor activation, a comparative SH2 domain interactome analysis (e.g., using SH2 domain arrays or affinity purification coupled with mass spectrometry) will be performed. By isolating proteins that bind in a phosphorylation-dependent manner (present with wild-type Kit, absent with the kinase-dead mutant), the method will directly identify and characterize the specific SH2 proteins whose phosphorylation or recruitment is a direct consequence of Kit's catalytic activity, filtering out non-specific interactors.

ideas.title: Systematically screen for alternative E3 ligases using a synthetic recruitment platform
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from synthetic biology, where combinatorial scaffolds are used to test enzyme variants for pathway optimization. The analogous problem of finding the most efficient enzyme for a synthetic task is solved by creating a decoupled, modular recruitment system. Adapted to this context, we propose building a synthetic recruitment platform to systematically test which E3 ubiquitin ligases, beyond c-Cbl, can be recruited by an antibody to ubiquitinate and degrade ErbB-2. This directly challenges the assumption that c-Cbl is the only relevant E3 ligase by enabling a controlled, high-throughput screen of alternative E3s in an identical cellular and recruitment context.

ideas.title: Quantitative mapping of lineage-specific Kit signaling thresholds to mutation severity landscapes
ideas.description: This research direction aims to quantitatively define the minimal Kit signaling output (threshold) required for the normal development and maintenance of erythrocyte, melanocyte, germ cell, and mast cell lineages. It then systematically maps how specific Kit or SLF mutations perturb signaling output relative to these lineage-specific thresholds, explaining the mutation's differential phenotypic severity. This approach transforms the question from 'do lineages differ?' to a predictive model: 'Given a mutation's quantitative impact on Kit signaling, can we forecast its lineage-specific pathological consequences based on established thresholds?' The synergy lies in using the threshold (Idea B) as the interpretative key for the differential severity (Idea A), enabling a principled, rather than observational, understanding of tissue-specific vulnerability.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal mapping of cell-autonomous versus non-autonomous Kit/SLF signaling functions in embryonic lineage specification
ideas.description: This research program integrates the goal of defining stage-specific embryonic functions of SLF/Kit signaling with the hypothesis-testing framework of its cell-autonomy. It aims to determine if the separate roles identified in early development (e.g., in primordial germ cell survival, melanoblast migration, and hematopoietic progenitor maintenance) are each mediated purely by cell-autonomous signaling within the Kit-expressing lineage, or if specific functions critically depend on non-cell-autonomous signaling from neighboring tissues. By employing temporally controlled genetic mosaics, tissue-specific ligand/receptor deletions, and live imaging in model embryos, the study will generate a functional map correlating specific developmental outcomes with their required signaling mechanism. This will resolve whether the Kit/SLF pathway is a versatile tool used in different 'modes' (autonomous vs. non-autonomous) for different purposes, fundamentally advancing our understanding of how a single ligand-receptor pair can orchestrate multiple, distinct morphogenetic events.

ideas.title: Quantifying lineage-specific dose-response to titrated Kit signaling in a unified progenitor model
ideas.description: This study directly tests the hypothesis that the four Kit-dependent lineages exhibit different quantitative requirements for Kit signaling during specification. Using a genetically uniform, in vitro progenitor population capable of generating all four lineages, we apply a titratable synthetic Kit ligand (e.g., a drug-dimerizable Kit receptor system) to provide identical signaling input. By measuring the differentiation efficiency to erythroid, melanocytic, germ cell-like, and mast cell fates across a precise gradient of Kit activation, we generate lineage-specific dose-response curves. This allows a rigorous, quantitative comparison of the minimal signaling amplitude and duration required for lineage commitment, moving beyond a vague threshold comparison.

ideas.description: This fused approach integrates a genome-wide yeast two-hybrid screen using the Kit cytoplasmic domain as bait with a parallel in vivo genetic modifier screen in a W/Sl mutant background. This strategy directly connects novel binding partners to their role in Kit-mediated signaling pathways in a living system. The result is a prioritized list of high-confidence genetic modifiers that likely act through direct physical interaction with the Kit receptor, accelerating the mapping of its functional signaling network.

ideas.title: Stress-Triggered Vulnerability: Unmasking Latent Adult Tissue Roles of Developmental Genes via Inducible Ablation
ideas.description: This redesigned study tests the hypothesis that germline mutations in key developmental genes like W (c-Kit) and Sl (Stem Cell Factor) lead to compensatory adaptations that conceal their essential functions in adult tissue maintenance. Using tamoxifen-inducible, tissue-specific Cre drivers to conditionally knockout these genes in adult mice, we bypass developmental effects. We then subject these animals and controls to a battery of physiological stressors—including partial hepatectomy, intestinal irradiation, skin wounding, and hematopoietic challenge—to probe for latent vulnerabilities in homeostasis and repair. This approach definitively separates developmental from adult functions and identifies the specific contexts in which these pathways remain critical.

ideas.description: This approach develops next-generation PROTAC-like molecules that not only recruit c-Cbl to specific oncogenic receptors but also actively monitor and maintain functional c-Cbl levels. The system uses a c-Cbl abundance sensor module, derived from known stabilizing interactors like Sprouty proteins, which is conditionally activated when free, active c-Cbl is depleted. This ensures sustained degradation of the target oncoprotein while preserving c-Cbl's homeostasis and its broader role in normal cellular regulation, thereby overcoming the critical negative feedback flaw of simple recruiters.

ideas.title: Testing Kit Signaling Autonomy via Engineered Cell-Autonomous Receptor Systems
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that Kit signaling effects in development require non-cell-autonomous interactions between lineages by adapting a solution from plant symbiotic biology. In that field, the question of whether nodulation signaling was purely cell-autonomous was resolved by engineering transgenic plants with modified, constitutively active Nod factor receptors that functioned independently of the microbial ligand. Adapting this, the proposed study would engineer a constitutively active, ligand-independent Kit receptor and express it in a lineage-restricted and inducible manner within a Kit-null background. If developmental programs (e.g., melanocyte migration/survival or hematopoietic stem cell maintenance) are rescued despite the absence of Kit ligand from the surrounding environment, it would demonstrate that Kit signaling is purely cell-autonomous for those processes. Conversely, a failure of rescue would reveal a necessary non-cell-autonomous component.

ideas.title: A Mammalian Membrane-Based Interaction Screen with Orthogonal Validation for Kit Cytoplasmic Domain Interactors
ideas.description: This redesigned approach first uses a mammalian membrane yeast two-hybrid (MYTH) system, which positions the Kit cytoplasmic domain as a bait at the yeast membrane via a transmembrane anchor, mimicking its native localization and reducing false positives from nuclear artifacts. Primary hits from this screen are then rigorously validated through two orthogonal methods in mammalian cells: co-immunoprecipitation to confirm the interaction occurs with full-length proteins in a complex cellular lysate, and bioluminescence resonance energy transfer (BRET) to demonstrate direct, proximity-based interaction in live cells. This sequential, multi-platform strategy ensures identified interactors are both specific and physiologically relevant, overcoming the key limitations of the classic yeast two-hybrid system.

ideas.title: Decode lineage-specific signaling differences by mapping the cellular 'wiring diagram'
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from electrical engineering, where the impact of a faulty component is understood by analyzing the complete circuit diagram. The analogous problem is determining why the same Kit or SLF mutation causes varying severity in different cell lineages. The adapted research idea is to map the complete 'signaling circuit' context for each lineage. This involves systematically profiling differences in pathway crosstalk, feedback mechanisms (e.g., phosphatases, miRNAs), expression of redundant receptors or ligands, and downstream effector networks. By building computational models of these lineage-specific wiring diagrams, the research aims to predict and explain how the mutation disrupts the network logic differently in each cellular context, moving beyond a linear view of the pathway.

ideas.title: Adapting Phage Display Screening for Yeast Two-Hybrid to Identify High-Affinity Kit Interactors
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Antibody Engineering—where phage display libraries are panned under increasingly stringent conditions to isolate high-affinity binders—to the context of a yeast two-hybrid screen for proteins interacting with Kit's cytoplasmic domain. The analogous solution involves iterative selection under competitive pressure. The adapted research idea is to perform the yeast two-hybrid screen not as a single assay, but as multiple iterative rounds of mating and selection on media supplemented with escalating concentrations of a competitive molecule (e.g., imatinib or a known Kit ligand). This stringent, multi-round screening protocol is designed to selectively enrich for yeast clones harboring prey proteins that form the most stable and high-affinity interactions with the Kit bait, filtering out weak or nonspecific binders that would be lost in a standard screen.

ideas.title: Quantitative Phenotype-to-Lesion Mapping via a Molecular Damage Scoring System
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from structural engineering, where the severity of macroscopic failure is systematically predicted from quantified material defects. The analogous problem in genetics is predicting phenotypic severity from molecular lesions. The adapted solution is to develop a 'Molecular Damage Score' that quantifies key parameters of the signaling defect caused by a W/Sl mutation (e.g., fold change in kinase activity, percent reduction in protein-protein binding). This quantitative score, derived from in vitro and cellular biophysical assays, will then be rigorously correlated with a multi-faceted phenotypic severity index measured in vivo. The outcome is a predictive, quantitative framework that maps the continuum of molecular lesion magnitude to the spectrum of phenotypic outcomes, moving beyond qualitative genotype-phenotype associations.

ideas.title: Apply Deep Mutational Scanning from Protein Engineering to Profile SH2 Domain Specificity
ideas.description: This idea adapts the deep mutational scanning (DMS) approach from protein engineering to profile SH2 domain binding specificity. In the analogous field, DMS is used to comprehensively map sequence-function relationships by screening vast mutant libraries with high-throughput sequencing. Adapted to this context, we will construct a DMS library encompassing all possible single (and potentially double) amino acid variants within the phosphorylated Kit peptide sequence. This library will be used in phage display selections against a purified SH2 domain of interest. Subsequent high-throughput sequencing of the enriched phage pools, compared to the pre-selection library, will yield a quantitative fitness landscape. This data will reveal not just the optimal binding motif, but the permissiveness or constraint at each peptide position, providing an unprecedented depth of specificity profiling for the SH2-phosphopeptide interaction.

ideas.title: Adapt Mosaic Analysis with Double Markers (MADM) from neurobiology to study pigment cell-autonomy
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting Mosaic Analysis with Double Markers (MADM), a technique pioneered in developmental neurobiology for high-resolution clonal analysis, to the study of pigment cell biology. The analogous solution uses Cre-loxP-mediated interchromosomal recombination to generate sparse, genetically distinct mosaic clones within an otherwise normal tissue, allowing for precise analysis of cell-autonomous gene function and clonal behavior. The adapted research idea applies this system to melanocytes to study classical coat color mutations like W (Kit) and Sl (Kitl). By creating mice where a small, traceable fraction of melanocytes is homozygous mutant (or wild-type) while the majority of tissue and microenvironment is of the opposite genotype, one can directly test the cell-autonomous requirements of these genes in melanocyte development, migration, and survival within the hair follicle niche, disentangling intrinsic cellular functions from extrinsic signaling roles.

ideas.title: Mechanistically dissect lineage-intrinsic factors driving differential vulnerability to Kit/SLF mutations using an isogenic hPSC platform
ideas.description: This redesigned study uses an isogenic set of human pluripotent stem cells (hPSCs) engineered with an inducible, lineage-traced Kit or SLF mutation, which are then differentiated into the relevant affected lineages (e.g., melanocytes, hematopoietic progenitors, interstitial cells of Cajal). Within this controlled genetic background, it employs live-cell biosensors and phospho-proteomics to quantitatively map how the mutation differentially disrupts dynamic signaling networks (MAPK, PI3K, JAK/STAT) in each lineage over time. This allows direct testing of whether differential vulnerability stems from lineage-specific signaling circuit architecture, baseline pathway flux, or compensatory pathway activation, moving beyond correlation to establish causal mechanisms.

ideas.title: Genome-wide modifier screen stratified by molecular lesion severity
ideas.description: This fused approach integrates a genome-wide genetic modifier screen with a pre-stratified panel of W/Sl mutants of known molecular lesion and quantified phenotypic severity. By performing the modifier screen in parallel across this stratified allelic series, we can systematically identify genetic interactions that are specific to certain molecular defects (e.g., loss-of-function vs. hypomorphic vs. neomorphic) rather than the general mutant phenotype. This reveals whether the genetic network buffering a pathway is robust to all perturbations or is exquisitely sensitive to the precise nature of the molecular lesion, uncovering context-dependent genetic vulnerabilities and resilience mechanisms.

ideas.title: Genome-wide epistasis mapping via combinatorial perturbation and high-content phenotyping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the combinatorial library screening approach from synthetic chemistry and materials science to perform a genome-wide modifier screen. In the analogous field, automated platforms create and test vast arrays of material compositions to find combinations that drastically alter a base property. Here, the W/Sl mutant phenotype is the 'base property.' A genome-wide library of genetic perturbations (e.g., RNAi or CRISPR) is systematically crossed into the W/Sl background in a high-throughput format. The solution is adapted by employing automated, high-content phenotypic analysis (e.g., multi-parameter imaging of relevant cellular structures) to quantitatively score each double-mutant, identifying genetic perturbations that suppress or enhance the original mutant defect. This allows for the efficient, large-scale mapping of epistatic interactions and genetic networks modifying the pathway of interest.

ideas.title: Employ a Synthetic Developmental Biology Pipeline with Modular Perturbation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from synthetic biology and metabolic engineering, where modular, inducible perturbation systems are used to test gene function at specific stages of multi-step biosynthesis pathways. The analogous solution involves constructing synthetic gene circuits for timed intervention. Adapted to the stem cell context, the research idea is to employ a staged, in vitro embryonic stem cell (ESC) differentiation model as a synthetic developmental pipeline. By integrating modular, temporally controlled perturbation tools—such as inducible CRISPRi or degron tags for Kit or Steel (SCF) components—Kit/Steel signaling can be turned ON or OFF with precision at defined lineage bifurcation points (e.g., during hematopoiesis versus melanocyte specification). This approach moves beyond correlation to enable causal mapping of Kit/Steel function to specific lineage commitment decisions within the differentiation model.

ideas.title: Parallel orthogonal genetic screens to isolate core pathway modifiers
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea involves conducting two independent genome-wide modifier screens in parallel. The first screen uses the original W/Sl mutant, while the second uses a different, non-allelic mutation that disrupts the same biological pathway but through a distinct molecular mechanism. The genetic modifiers identified from each screen are then computationally intersected. This intersection yields a refined list of high-confidence hits that are general suppressors or enhancers of the pathway's dysfunction, filtering out background-specific synthetic interactions and thereby revealing the pathway's core genetic architecture.
